title: Network Topologies: The Blueprint
description: Explore physical and logical layouts: Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, and Hybrid designs.

slides.title: What is a Network Topology? 🗺️
slides.content:
# The Layout of the Digital City

A **network topology** defines how devices are physically or logically connected. It's the **blueprint** that dictates data flow, redundancy, and scalability.

There are two views:
- **Physical Topology:** The actual cable paths, switch placements, and device locations. (Where the wires go).
- **Logical Topology:** The path data actually takes through the network. (How the signals flow).

**Analogy:** Think of a city map.
- **Physical Topology** = The physical roads and intersections.
- **Logical Topology** = The one-way streets and traffic light patterns.

Understanding topologies is critical for **network design** and **troubleshooting**. A broken cable in a Bus topology takes down the whole network. A broken cable in a Star topology only affects one device.

quizData.question: What is the difference between physical and logical topology?

options.text: Physical is for wired; logical is for wireless.
Physical is the actual layout; logical is how data flows.

quizData.explanation: Correct! Physical topology is the hardware layout; logical topology defines the data path.

slides.title: Bus Topology 🚌
slides.content:
# The Original Shared Highway

**Description:** All devices connect to a single central cable (the **bus** or **backbone**).

**How it works:**
- A device sends a signal down the cable.
- **All devices** receive the signal, but only the intended recipient processes it.
- **Terminators** at each end absorb the signal to prevent reflection (echo).

**Pros:**
- Cheap and simple (minimum cable length).
- Easy to add new nodes.

**Cons (And Why We Don't Use It Anymore):**
- **Single Point of Failure:** Cut the cable anywhere, and the **entire network goes down**.
- **Performance:** Only one device can transmit at a time (Collision Domain). High traffic = high collisions = slow network.
- **Security:** Every device sees every packet (sniffing paradise).

**Legacy Tech:** 10BASE2 (Thinnet) and 10BASE5 (Thicknet) Ethernet.

slides.title: Ring Topology 💍
slides.content:
# The Token Passing Circle

**Description:** Each device connects to exactly two other devices, forming a **closed loop**.

**How it works:**
- Data travels in one direction around the ring.
- A special frame called a **Token** circulates. Only the device holding the Token is allowed to transmit.

**Pros:**
- **No collisions** (Token prevents them).
- Predictable performance under heavy load.

**Cons:**
- **Single Point of Failure:** One device or cable break breaks the ring. (Dual Ring / FDDI fixes this with a counter-rotating ring for redundancy).
- **Latency:** Each device adds a tiny delay as it regenerates the signal.

**Modern Usage:** **SONET/SDH** (fiber optic carrier networks) and **Industrial Control Systems** (Token Ring is still used in some factory floors for deterministic timing).

slides.title: Star Topology ⭐
slides.content:
# The Modern Standard (Ethernet)

**Description:** All devices connect to a **central device** (Switch or Hub).

**How it works:**
- Device A wants to talk to Device B.
- It sends the frame to the **central switch**.
- The switch looks at the Destination MAC and forwards it **only** to the port where Device B is connected.

**Pros:**
- **Fault Isolation:** A broken cable only affects the attached device, not the whole network.
- **Easy Troubleshooting:** Just check the link light on the switch port.
- **Scalability:** Add more switches to expand.

**Cons:**
- **Central Point of Failure:** If the **Switch** fails, everything connected to it goes down. (Mitigated by redundant power supplies and stacking).
- **Cable Cost:** Requires a dedicated cable run from each device to the switch closet.

**This is the topology of almost every office LAN in the world.**

quizData.question: Which topology provides the BEST fault isolation (a cut cable only affects one device)?
quizData.explanation: In a Star topology, each device has its own dedicated connection to the central switch, so a cable break is isolated.

slides.title: Mesh Topology 🕸️
slides.content:
# The Ultimate Redundancy

**Description:** Every device connects to **every other device** (Full Mesh) or multiple other devices (Partial Mesh).

**How it works:**
- Multiple redundant paths exist between any two points.
- If one link fails, data automatically reroutes through another path.

**Pros:**
- **Extreme Fault Tolerance:** Survives multiple link failures.
- **High Bandwidth:** Traffic can be load-balanced across multiple paths.

**Cons:**
- **Cost:** `n(n-1)/2` connections for a full mesh. 10 devices = 45 cables and 45 ports per device! **Insanely expensive.**
- **Complexity:** Requires routing protocols (OSPF, BGP) to manage the multiple paths.

**Where is it used?**
- **WAN Backbones:** The Internet core is a **partial mesh** of routers.
- **Data Center Spine-Leaf:** A structured partial mesh for high throughput.
- **Wireless Mesh:** Wi-Fi nodes relaying data to each other to extend coverage.

slides.title: Hybrid Topology 🧬
slides.content:
# The Real World is a Mix

Most enterprise networks are **Hybrid Topologies** combining Star, Mesh, and sometimes Ring.

**Example: A Corporate Campus**
- **Access Layer:** **Star** (End devices connect to Access Switches).
- **Distribution Layer:** **Partial Mesh** (Access switches connect redundantly to Core Switches).
- **Core Layer:** **Full Mesh** (Core routers connect to each other for maximum speed and redundancy).
- **WAN Edge:** **Ring** (Connecting remote branch offices via MPLS).

**Cybersecurity Insight:** Understanding topology helps in **threat modeling**. An attacker with physical access to a Bus network can sniff everything. An attacker with access to a Star switchport can only sniff traffic on their VLAN (unless they perform VLAN Hopping).

slides.title: Logical Topology: VLANs 🏷️
slides.content:
## The Invisible Overlay

Even though the **physical topology** might be a Star (everyone plugged into the same switch), the **logical topology** can be completely different using **VLANs (Virtual LANs)**.

A VLAN can make devices on different floors behave as if they are on the same cable (logical Bus), or segment a single switch into multiple isolated virtual switches.
